summary:
Mary Queen of Scots (1542–1587 AD) was one of history's most romantic and tragic royal figures — a queen who lost her throne to Protestant rebels, spent 19 years imprisoned by her cousin Elizabeth I of England, and was ultimately executed for her alleged complicity in a plot against the English crown. Mary was Queen of Scotland from six days old, was raised and educated at the French court, briefly became Queen of France (1559–1560), and returned to Scotland as a Catholic queen ruling a country that had just adopted Protestantism under John Knox's influence. Her three marriages were disastrous: her first husband Francis II died young; her second, Lord Darnley, was murdered (possibly with Mary's knowledge) in 1567; her third, the Earl of Bothwell (widely believed guilty of Darnley's murder), made her deeply unpopular.

Mary's abdication under Scottish Protestant pressure in 1567 and subsequent flight to England in 1568 created an impossible problem for [[event:2471|Elizabeth I]]. Mary had a legitimate claim to the English throne as Henry VIII's granddaughter; English Catholics saw her as rightful Queen of England; Elizabeth could neither safely release her nor easily justify executing an anointed queen. For nearly two decades, Mary was kept in comfortable captivity while being the focus of successive Catholic plots. The Babington Plot (1586), in which Mary appeared to endorse a plan to assassinate Elizabeth, provided the justification for her execution on February 8, 1587 — an act that horrified Catholic Europe and directly contributed to Philip II's decision to launch the [[event:2339|Spanish Armada]]. Ironically, Mary's son James VI of Scotland succeeded Elizabeth as James I of England in 1603, uniting the kingdoms.
